response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: You know, I don't spend much time on social media, maybe just a few times each month when my children send me something or when I want to see photos of the grandchildren. But even in those brief visits, I've noticed how confusing things can be with all this fake content floating around. At sixty-seven, working part-time as a carpenter here in Lecce, I've learned that building something solid requires knowing your materials, and the same goes for information, really. When I see something online that seems off, I've developed a habit of asking my family about it first. My four children are much more savvy with these things than I am, and they help me understand what's real and what's nonsense. I'm naturally curious, so when something catches my attention, I want to talk about it with people I trust rather than just believing it immediately. That's my first rule, I suppose. Talk to real people you know, not strangers on the internet. My husband and I often discuss what we see, and between us, we usually figure out if something smells fishy. I think the biggest problem is that people treat social media like it's the morning newspaper, but it's not. Anyone can post anything, and there's no editor checking facts like there used to be. In my carpentry work, if I used rotten wood, the whole piece would fall apart. Same principle applies here. You need to check your sources. I don't have fancy education, no formal schooling really, but common sense goes a long way. When something seems too dramatic or makes you very angry very quickly, that's usually a sign to step back. My Hindu beliefs teach me about discernment and not being swept away by illusions, and I think that applies perfectly to this modern problem. I also limit my time online precisely because I know I can't always tell what's true, and frankly, I'd rather spend my time baking or painting anyway. Those hobbies give me real satisfaction, not the anxiety that comes from scrolling through questionable content. My advice would be simple: don't rush to believe or share anything, ask people you trust in person, and remember that if you're only checking social media a few times monthly like me, you're probably better off than those glued to their screens all day.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 67-year-old Italian woman from Lecce with no formal education, working part-time as a carpenter. Her limited social media use (few times monthly) and high extraversion suggest she prefers real-world social interactions. Her curiosity drives her to investigate but her low openness means she relies on familiar, trusted sources rather than exploring new verification methods. Her moderate agreeableness and neuroticism suggest practical concern without excessive worry. Her Hindu beliefs can inform her perspective on discernment. Her hobbies (baking, painting) and carpentry work provide practical metaphors. Her four children likely help her navigate technology. The tone should be conversational, warm, practical, and grounded in real-life experience rather than technical knowledge.
response_parsed.tone: conversational, warm, practical, grounded in personal experience, slightly skeptical but not alarmist, uses everyday analogies from work and hobbies
